regions.label: text
regions.text:
Mrs. Sandy Morgan, 2615A Morgan, is in People's Hospital for treatment.

Mr. John A. Coleman, 4059 Finney avenue is very ill at People's Hospital.

Fisk Jubilee Singers at St. Paul Oct. 18, auspices. Helping Hand Club. Adv.

Mr. Cornellus Wilson of 1101 Glasgow avenue is confined to his bed with paralysis.

Dr. Mrs. Herrings returned last Monday after spending five weeks in the East.

Mrs. J. Stipton, 435 Gerfield, has returned from Chicago where she had a very pleasant visit with her children.

Miss Sallie B. Harding of Fort Wayne Indiana is visiting her father Mr. J. W. Harding of 2104 Clarke Ave.

Mr. and Mrs. C. L. Parker of Los Angeles, Cal. are the home guests of Mr. James Randall of 4230 W. North Market street.

W. K. Drew of Springfield proprietor of the University Pool Hallway in St. Louis on business and valued the Arms while here.

Miss Ethel Cornell returned to St. Louis Thursday, Sept 21 after a six-month visit to Seattle, Washington and other points west.

Mrs. Mary Fleming of Denver, Col. on route to Birmingham, Aka, stopped in the city to visit Mrs. Emma J. Clopton, 4202 Finney.

Miss Mamie L. Powell of 6509 McPearson avenue has returned to the city after having spent a very pleasant vacation in Michigan.

Miss Mae Low Hardie, the daughter of Mrs. and Mrs. J. F. Hardie, 2721 Clark Avenue left Tuesday night, Sept 20, 6628 Rever Howard University.

Mrs. E. L. Gray of 3932a Finney avenue announces the engagement of her daughter Ellen James Wood to Mr. James Robertson of Chicago, Illinois.

Rev S. H. Jones, pastor of the A. M. E. church, Albion, Ill. stopped in the city as guest of Mr. and Mrs. H. W. Stanton on his way from the Conference of January.

Mr. Fred F. Thompson, Linotype Operator of the Robinson and Harker Printing Co., returned to the city this week from an extended shift in the west.

Miss Areatha Henkal, R., of 1022 W. Helle Plate, entered Monday night in Glencoe of Mrs. M. E. Church, Albion, Ill. stopped in the city as guest of Mr. and Mrs. H. W. Stanton on his way from the Conference of January.

Mr. and Mrs. William C. Moten of 6714 Idaho Avenue announce the birth of a baby boy on Friday Sept 22. John Conway and mother are getting along nicely.

P. Hawkins, Assistant Chairman.

Rev W. Westmoreland, Pastor of Lambton Memorial C. M. E. Church, 413 South Jefferson Ave., Palazed 1905 in a recent rally.

L. Elliston Secretary.

Mrs. Lester Ewing of Racine Wis. is the guest of Mr. and Mrs. R. S. McWorter, 4442 Enright avenue. Mrs. Ewing has been the recipient of many social courtesies while here.

Mr. and Mrs. Bernard Hopson of N. Market St. returned to their home Sunday after spending a very pleasant week with Dr. and Mrs. Ware and attending the fair at Springfield.

Mrs. Glenn Green departed Sept 20 for Howard University, accompanied by her mother and sister. Mrs. U. Clarke also Miss Mae Low Hardie a former student of Howard. Mrs. Ollen will be in Washington indefinitely.

Mrs. and Mrs. S. J. Patton, 4016 W. Belle Place, mounted to Keokuk Iowa September 15, 1922, visiting Mr. Patton's young brother. Their stay was shortened by the death of Mrs. Patton's father, W. L. Pinken.

Mrs. John Means of 3126 Franklin avenue and her sister, Miss Lizzie Hamilton of Pulton Mo., who have been visiting relatives at Los Angeles, Cal., also visited Punchee, Oak, Salt Lake City and Kansas City. The ladies returned to their home last week.

Mr. Joseph Bangs son of Mr. and Mrs. C. J. Banks, 4577 Lucky Street, entertained Tuesday afternoon from 9:30 in honor of his fourth birthday. There were 14 guests. The honor of grazing the table was placed upon Master Dallas Lucas. Many usefpl gifts were received and all departed delighted.

Mrs. Claretha M. Barrett after an absence of 108 days has returned to the city. While away she perfected the organization of the "Tribe of the Queen of Shakra" of the United Sons and Daughters of Ethiopia in Gary, Ind., and visited several other cities in northern Illinois and Indiana Iowa interested group leaders and workers in each one.

Mr. and Mrs. Norman R. Mason of 1120 North Compton avenue entertained at dinner Monday evening. Mr. and Mrs. Fate Marable, Miss Annie Ruth McKinney, Miss Gena Hobbs, Mr. David Martin, Mr. Bernard Rolley, Mr. Mason departed Tuesday morning for New Orleans. Ms. Mason will join him later to be gone for the re-

regions.label: text
regions.text:
Mrs. P. E. Thompson of 2121 Walnut is still confined to her bed.

Fisk Jubilee Singers at St. Paul Oct. 16, anspices Helping Hand Club — Adv.

If you are full of pep and like to step, do it with the Cassandra Girls Oct. 6 at Pythian Hall — Adv.

Oh! Look who's coming again on Oct. 20. It is the loving Great Western Boys. — Adv.

Mrs. J. P. Brown of 3441n Alberta entertained Saturday evening in honor of her daughter's Mrs. A. O. Thornton wedding anniversary.

Mrs. Carrie A. West of 5455 Delmar had as her guest her brother, Rev. Luther L. Holloway of Buffalo, N. Y.

The "Phedelema Girls" will observe their tent anniversary Saturday afternoon at Miss Edna Bord, 4368 Enright Avenue.

Word has been received that Miss Maurice Williams and Miss Grace Nichols are selling ahead ship ward bound from Europe.

Mr and Mrs Wm. Mitchell formerly of 3208 Pine St. are now domiciled in their new home, 2620 Pine St. Phone Bounty 1766 R.

Mrs. Lincien Garrett of 4582 Garfield entertained with a whist Saturday in honor of her sister, Mrs. Robert Reagin of Selma, Ala.

Miss Lillian Miller of Sullivan Indiana returned to St. Louis and will reside at Our home of her uncle R. Manuel, 4107 Finney Avenue.

The La Cove Cent Club will be the guest of Mrs. Ethel Bowles, 4254 Enright, on Saturday afternoon. The Sonora Club met with Mr. Anderson, 4045 Cook on Thursday afternoon.

The Union Mothercraft meeting will be held at First Baptist Church Friday October 6 at 2 p.m. Mrs. Hall of Mother Craft Council will speak.

Fisk Jubilee Singers at St. Paul Oct. 16, anspices. Helping Hand Club — Adv.

Mrs. Jefferson of 3527 Lawton will be temporarily honored at 3422 Las Clare until Oct. 17th. Phone Lindell 2655W. After that time she will be in Chicago.

Don't forget the Cassandra Girls first full dance Ogr & with Fate Marable — Adv.

Ruth, George, Helen and Elizabeth Coleman have returned to St. Riga a context after appealing a delightful gift with their mother, Mrs. B Coleman of 4255 West Finney avenue.

The Cassandra Girls say come at 8 dance until late with Mrs. Fate Oct. 6 — Adv.

Mr. Willie Tardier of San Diego Calle has been visiting his cousin, Mrs. Ida Island of 916 Iron Street, for the last ten days. Mr and Mrs Bland wish to thank their friends for helping to make his glass a pleasant one.

Mrs. C. H. Phillips entertained at lunch on Saturday, Mr. and Mrs. R. W. S. Johnson of Windsor, Canada and Mrs. W. H. King of Atlanta Other guests were Nicandra Josie H. R. Evans, W. O. McMahon, Widefaced Jacobs and Nolie Agee.

Mrs. Harold Jones of 4533 Enright Ave. and her mother, Mrs. Frank L. Williams, were At Home to a number of their friends during the week in honor of Mrs. John R. Pinkett who with wife, wn to Atlanta Gil. He resided Mrs. Jones was also hostess to the Dames Thursday afternoon, in their friendship of the season.

What will happen Nov. 11th? Adv.

The Delphine Girls Club held their regular meeting at the residence of Miss Vibiah Marrows, 2661 Lensus Tuesday, September 21st Miss L. Cowl was greatly received as a member. After the routine of business a delicious request was served. Watch for our Knoll Dunce.

regions.label: text
regions.text:
Watch this space in the next week's lesson.

The grateful thanks of the Board of Management and Secretary, Mrs. Mattie Dover Young, is given the promoters of one of the most entailable and highly literary affairs of the season, viz. the poetical contest. Mrs. W. C. Bridges as a manager and leader has few equals. Mrs. Walter Gilles in the musical program gave evidence of rare judgment and taste. Misses Maude Kennedy, Harrick Hutt, Jujana Greentee, Odessa Burton, Edith Banks, Ella Louise Pitte, each in their rendition of very difficult poems deserve especial mention, giving promise of much ability and evidence of excellent training. Churches and the number of tickets sold by each will be given in the next issue.

regions.text: IMPORTANT ANNOUNCEMENT

regions.label: text
regions.text:
A class in vocational methods, number Smith-Smughes net for training of teachers in evening part-time and grade-manual schools, will be organized by the Board of Education at Marshall School. Tuesday, Oct. 2, 3:30 p./m.
This class is similar to the one

regions.label: text
regions.text:
The Fran Frans, one of the pioneer ladies' social clubs in the city, met with Mrs W. C. Maxwell, 4254 Lucky street on Thursday of last week and disbanded indefinitely. This is the club that, in a spirit of patriotism, when our boys were called to France, abandoned its teutonic name, took up the chase of the Kaiser and was known as the "Thursday Club." The original name was reclaimed after the armistice.

In harmony with its many past activities for charity, the members donated the final contents of its treasury, $17.00, to the People's Hospital.

regions.label: text
regions.text: Montgomery City, Mo. Sept. 21, 1922. Lincoln Park School opened the eleventh instant with an enrollment of 72. All clubs have been organized and are doing good work. The pig, poultry and banking clubs are making substantial progress, and the sewing club under the leadership of Mrs. E. D. Taylor is now chartered and registered as a standard club of the Agricultural Department of the State University F. S. BOWLES. Principall.

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: MME. B. RHODA IN CITY

regions.label: text
regions.text: Mme. B. (Rhoda, President: Rhdao Beauty Culture College, Nashville, Tenn. is in the city after visiting Chicago Detroit and New York. She is a wide awake business woman and lectures. Mme. will speak at Bethel A. M. E. Church, Wellston, Mo., Thursday, sept. 5th and at Mt. Olive Missionary Church, 17th and Wash. Wed. October 4th, 1922.

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: BIRTHDAY PARTY GIVEN

regions.label: text
regions.text:
A beautifully arranged birthday party and luncheon was given in honor of Mr. L. Riley by Mr. and Mrs. J. Campbell at their home, No. 7, South 23rd St. Last Sunday.

In addition to the family the following congenial company was present. Mr. and Mrs. C. W. Watson; Mr. and Mrs. Tobe White, Mr. and Mrs. Bonds; Miss Mary Newton; Mr. Robert Nailing, Mr. Mose Elders; Mr. B. Dennis, Mr. George Golins and Mr. Otis Lawson.

Following the four course luncheon the guests were entertained with music by Mr. Otis Lawson and Mr. George Golins.

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: INFORMATION WANTED.

regions.label: text
regions.text: Any one knowing the whereabouts of Ignis Christian, last heard of was in St. Louis in 1918, will please notify the Ariens office. Some important information awaits him. (9.29-2)

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: Tucker's Business Institute

regions.label: text
regions.text: Monday, September 15th, the school opened with many new students enrolled for the Business Training Course. It has been necessary to open a morning class this year for the first time. The hips are 10.5 m., to 12.0 m.; every day except Saturday. Exciting classes: Tuesday, Thursday, and Fridays from 2.0 m., to 6.0 m. The terms are REASONABLE, small payment down, balance in monthly payments. Students are constantly enrolling. Persons interested in taking up the Business Course are urged to enroll immediately before classes have advanced too far. Among those who have recently enrolled are: Hazel Brown, David Essay, Amine K. Ford, Margaret Haley, Blanche Hamilton, Kittle Henson, Harriett Honson, Elmer Hubbard, Lillie Hindson, Robert Jackson, Lavinia Johnson, Francis McMurran, Striker Mason, Geraldine Murray, Janita North, Ruby Oega, Victoria Rasson, Davis Scott, Alice Strzker, Alberta Talierro, Altemae Williams, Margie J. C. Wilson.

regions.label: text
regions.text:
SERVICES FOR THE DEAF
AT ALL SAINTS' CHURCH
The Colored deaf of St. Louis and vicinity are cordially invited to meet at All Saints Church at 2 P. M. Sunday, October 1st and 5th.
There will be a service and sermon in the sign language. After the service plans will be discussed looking to future services and a church school for the Colored deaf.

regions.label: text
regions.text:
BY THOM. ATKINS.

Labor is the cure for the fils of life:
While one is tired, there's no time for strife;
Real life is found in lowly toll.
When hands and face we'll dare to soil.

Labor stills ones passions and directs his mind;
It makes one thoughtful, gentle and kind;
It brings in lack every sense to bear.
And banishes every wondd thought or slavish fear.

PRIVATE INSTRUCTION
READING, WRITING,
ARITHMETIC and LANGUAGE
Write New For Appointments
Adams Bldg. 1 & V.
St. Louis Army

regions.label: text
regions.text: NOTICE TO PUBLIC

regions.label: text
regions.text: I wish to inform my many friends and patrons that I have returned to the city and resumed my undertaking duties after an absence of five months, due to the illness of my wife, who I am pleased to say, has regained her health. I will be ready to be of service when required.

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text:
OLIVE CHAPEL A. M. E. CHURCH,
KIRKWOOD.

regions.label: text
regions.text:
The sick of our church are still improving nicely. Mrs. E. D. Wheeler of West Monroe and Mr. Patrick Ming both are able to be up and do light work.

The people of Kirkwood are yet talking of the great meeting the men had on Men's Day two weeks ago.

Our services were very good last Sunday. The pastor preached from St. sMitt. 11:30. Text: For yoke is easy and burden light.

At 3 p. m. in the pastor, in company with his wife, wen't to Rev. S. R. Stanley's Harvest Home Program.

The Allen League was called to order by the president, Mrs. S. A. Fish-bask, at 7 p. m. It was a fine meeting and was discussed by quite a few of the people that were present.

Stewardess Board No. 2 will have their baby contest Thursday evening at the residence of Mrs. Jane Davis Sept. 29. We trust the entire community will attend. October 1st is our fourth and last quarterly meeting for this conference year. We continually invite the general public to come and assist us with their prayers and finances.

October 6th the Ladies' Aid will have a quilt contest at our church

regions.label: text
regions.text:
October 13th the Trustee Helpers will give a high-class entertainment at our church:
Detecther is Women's Day at Olive Chapel
Stewardess Board No. 2 entertained Stewardess Board No. 1. Lane-Tabernacle C. M. E. Church. Wednesday and Thursday evenings.
The Lookout Committee of our church also had a fine entertainment Saturday evening at the residence of Mrs. Susie Smith, Meacham Park.
Our Inventory Home services will be the 12th of October.
We are doing tangible work in Olive Chapel, and if you would like to be one in the number, come over and unite with us.

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: HAT SALE

regions.label: text
regions.text: Beaver and Felt Hats on sale at cost Friday and Saturday, Sept 29, and 30 at the industrial Dressmaking Shop. 4316 East Avenue. (Adv.)

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: Y. M. C. A. BOY'S DIVISION OPENS FALL PROGRAM

regions.label: text
regions.text:
The first fall class in Pine Street Y. M. C. A. Boy's Division and Elliardsville Y. M. C. A. Club for Boys will be held Monday October 2nd. This day will be in the finest program for boys ever offered by these Departments and one of the finest four-fold programs being offered any boys anywhere.

The "Midgets" (under 13 years) will be organized into "Ys Trail Hangers" the "Juniora" will be organized into Tribes of "Pioneers" with the world famous Christian Citizenship Training Program to guide it. Both programs will consist of handcraft, scout craft, woodcraft, camp craft, and intellectual, physical, social and religious activities.

The Seniors (16 to 18) will have a great time in their Senior Triangle Club. They will have three gymnastics and swimming classes a class in "Older Boys Problems and Christian Principles", health classes conducted by physletans, orchestra, gymtanks, basket ball, hiking, camping and other fine features.

Many other clubs will be formed later in the Fall. The Summer Older Boys Triangle Club is among them.

The boys will have a big part in the opening exhibition on Friday, October 6th, and they hope to see all of their friends and parents out.

The Cafeteria

The increased demand on our Cafeteria makes it necessary for us to consider possible expansion. A special meeting of the board of Directors is called tonight to discuss plans for an additional service department. It will be of interest to report that the receipts for the past month have been the largest of any previous month and last Sunday, was the largest day on record. With it a very short time we hope to be able to accommodate our patrons in a much larger way.

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text:
REV. J. K. PARKER RETURNS
FROM TRAVELS IN SOUTH

regions.label: text
regions.text:
Rev. J. K. Parker has returned from his trip south where he had gone to attend the funeral of the late Dr. E. C. Morris, after which he visted Greenville and Luhi, Miss, and Memphis, Tenn.

The pastor was very ill with chills and fever while at Greenville. Through the excellent care of Rev. Parker's piece, Miss Evelyn Woods, and the attention given him by Rev. Lindsey and the Rev. J. H. Davenport and family, Rev. Parker was brought back to good health again.

The members of Pleasant Green Baptist Church wish to thank these friends through the Argus for the kindness shown their pastor while he was in a distant land.

Pleasant Green will hold its fall revival October 4th to November 5, 1922. The allied forces of the Kingdom of Christ are called upon to rally to this battlefield to help win a victory for our Lord.

Prayer service will be going on night and day during the revival services. Each soldier is asked to meet at the church every Sunday morning between the hours of 5 a. m. and 7:20 a. m. to receive sufficient grace to put over this program for God and his Christ.

regions.label: text
regions.text: A superb cast of fifty trained artists—Special scenery designed by Mr. Geo. W. Jaeger, the noted painter. Magnificent oriental costumes, charming musical numbers that are catchy and classy. A bevy of beautiful girls richly attired. A "Wedding Chorus" led by Mrs. Zelle Cole Evans and trained by Mr. J. Gerald Tyler. Sixteen dainty little Chinese children at "Wedding Feast."

regions.label: text
regions.text:
and officers, held its monthly meeting Wednesday evening, Sept. 20, at the beautiful residence of Mr. A. L. Gardner, 2825 Walnut street, and teacher of the great Morning Star Class. It was one of the most enjoyable meetings of the year. There were 21 present. The meeting was called to order by Sept. Elmer E. Phelps at 9 o'clock p. m. After a few brief remarks by the superintendent, it was decided to make it a grand social affair, after which we were served with the most delicious refreshments. This meeting will long be remembered.

The faculty was very much honored by having Clemnie Stumps, cousin of Mr. Gardner, and Mr. E. E. Phelps, superintendent, render their services as waiters on this occasion. Among those present were: Mr. and Mrs. E. B. Robinson, Mr. and Mrs. L. J. Lewis, Dr. and Mrs. N. O. Bracy, Measlesmets B. B. Oliver, Ella Howell, A. Brown, Mattie Murphy, Misses Katie Smith, Cecille Oliver, Marjorie Scott, Lottie Middlebrooks, Nell Howell, Jennie Tyler, Messrs. Robert E. Wright, L. B. Reed, Jackson Bracy, A. L. Gardner, E. E. Phelps and Fred Carter, Mrs. Shepherd, Gardner, Mistress of the home, came in and greeted the teachers before they departed.

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: PILGRIMS FREE BAPTIST CHURCH

regions.label: text
regions.text:
Our early prayer and praise services are much enjoyed by all present. The Holy Spirit meets us there and is with us throughout the day's services. The Christian Endeavor gave their first contest rally in the afternoon for the benefit of the church. Total collected $73.35. The rally was well attended. The winner of the contest prize was Sister Rosie Mackey. She was awarded $10.00 in gold. The president of the Christian Endeavor is doing her best toward finance. Every part of the church is spiritually alive. The Willing Workers Club will have a one-week autumn fair at the church beginning-Oct. 2. Come and have a jolly good time. A short program each night.

SISTER IDA BENTON.
Pres.
SISTER ROSIE MACKEY.
Secy.
REV. M. LAFONT.
Pastor.

SNOWS COLLEGE OF DRESS-MAKING.

A thorough course of instruction covering every phase of dressmaking. The latest, up-to-date methods, shortest time, about eight weeks. Reasonable terms. Satisfaction guaranteed. 2836 Olive St. Bomont 407.
MME. EVA HAMILTON, Mgr.

Ker, physician and surgeon, will appear to his nephews and friends that on and after October 1, 1922, his office will be moved from the Midway Building, 2348 Olive, corner Jefferson to his new location No. 4 South Comptop Ave. corner Laclede Ave. over Brazelton's Drug Store. Same, Bell Phone. Bounty 2545, Kirkon Central 2861.
Hours 10 to 11 a.m.; 2 to 4 p.m.; 6 to 8 p.m. Sundays 1 to 3 p.m. his residence, 4230 W Flaney, corner Pendleton, remains the same where he will receive patients 6 to 10 a.m.; 4 to 6 p.m. and at night.

regions.label: text
regions.text: Beginning October 1st, by increasing the number of our beds, we shall be able to accommodate about 10 more men, between the ages of 18-30 years. Rooms are open to members and may be secured by having names put on our waiting list.
